summary: Target's store network positions it as the preferred physical retail partner for digitally native brands that want to avoid Amazon
summary_extended: Baker argues that Target's extensive physical store network gives it a structural advantage as the preferred brick-and-mortar distribution partner for digitally native and DTC brands that are unwilling to sell through Amazon due to data and brand-control concerns, and cannot afford to build their own store footprints. By offering exclusive distribution deals, Target lowers these brands' customer acquisition costs (CAC) while improving their LTV/CAC ratios, creating a mutually beneficial relationship that locks in a curated, exclusive supplier pipeline. Baker contends this pipeline will serve as the foundation for Target's own third-party marketplace — a high-margin e-commerce layer built on top of its omnichannel infrastructure — positioning Target to compete in marketplace economics without relying on Amazon's ecosystem. This is framed as a forward-looking, long-term structural thesis drawing on Baker's venture-side experience with DTC brands, with the marketplace layer described as something that is 'going to be' built rather than already realized as of late 2020.
detail: Many DTC/digitally native brands do not want to sell through Amazon due to data concerns and cannot afford their own stores. Target offers exclusive distribution deals, lowering these brands' CAC and improving their LTV/CAC. This exclusive supply pipeline will form the foundation of a curated third-party marketplace — the highest-margin form of e-commerce.
confidence_evidence: Baker draws on venture-side experience with DTC brands and frames this as a forward-looking strategic thesis; described as 'going to be' rather than already realized

ticker_audit.reasoning: The entire thesis is specifically about Target's strategic positioning as a physical retail partner for DTC brands and its path to building a high-margin third-party marketplace; TGT is the direct subject.
